content.decision_question: Should fiscal conditionality be pursued as a lower-conflict alternative to direct admissions or structural reform, given that no evidence currently demonstrates its causal effect on segregation outcomes?
content.evidence_boundary: Piloting fiscal conditionality and measuring maintainer responses (including gaming behavior and diversity outcome shifts) could clarify whether this lever produces measurable segregation reduction. It cannot resolve, in advance of such evidence, whether the value of avoiding legal/political conflict with maintainers justifies accepting a currently unproven mechanism over more direct but more contentious reforms.
content.tension: Tying funding premiums to equity outcomes avoids the centralized-admissions-versus-free-choice legal tension and could operate without direct mandates on maintainers, but its causal effectiveness on segregation is entirely unproven, and maintainers could game non-SES criteria in ways that harm other groups, raising the question of whether an indirect, lower-conflict lever is worth pursuing absent evidence of impact.
content.title: Fiscal conditionality as an untested indirect lever versus direct structural or admissions reform
value_poles: minimizing direct governance conflict with maintainer autonomy
ensuring reforms have demonstrable causal impact on segregation
